summary: Scottish novelist and poet, often considered the inventor of the historical novel. Known for Ivanhoe, Waverley, Rob Roy, The Heart of Midlothian, and The Bride of Lammermoor. Mark Twain blamed Scott's influence for causing the American Civil War.

works.name: Ivanhoe
works.description: Scott's most famous novel, in which the protagonist disguises himself as the knight 'Desdichado' (the Disinherited Knight) after returning from the Crusades. He defeats the Templar Brian de Bois-Guilbert at a joust and bestows 'Queen of the Tournament' honor upon Lady Rowena. The Jewish moneylender Isaac of York is ordered arrested by Bois-Guilbert. Ivanhoe defends the Jewish Rebecca from being burned as a witch; Bois-Guilbert dies in a trial by combat. Ulrica helps save the title character. Ivanhoe is disinherited by his father Cedric 'the Saxon.' The swineherd Gurth wears a brass collar with a Saxon inscription. Thomas Carlyle praises Gurth's collar in Past and Present. The novel inspired Southern chivalric culture, jousting tournaments in Chesnutt's The House Behind the Cedars, and a cross-burning scene in Thomas Dixon's The Clansman.

clues.clue: Protagonist disguises as Desdichado; defeats Brian de Bois-Guilbert; marries Rowena; defends Rebecca
examples: a knight disguises himself as Desdichado

clues.clue: Inspired Southern chivalric culture; Twain blamed Scott for causing the Civil War
examples: 'in great measure responsible for' the Civil War
the South's backwardness on this author's namesake disease

works.name: Waverley
works.description: Scott's first novel, often regarded as the first historical novel. The protagonist Edward visits the outlaw Donald Bean Lean's hideout and Flora MacIvor's castle, inspiring him to take up the Jacobite cause. Scott's subsequent novels were published 'by the author of Waverley.' A wrecked steamboat in Huckleberry Finn is named after Scott.

clues.clue: First historical novel; protagonist joins Jacobite cause after visiting Donald Bean Lean and Flora MacIvor
examples: Edward gets involved in the Jacobite Rising

clues.clue: Subsequent novels published 'by the author of Waverley'; steamboat named for Scott in Huckleberry Finn
examples: his subsequent books were published under the pseudonym 'by the author of' his first novel

works.name: Other Novels
works.description: The Heart of Midlothian: Jeanie Deans walks barefoot to London to seek a pardon for her sister Effie from Queen Caroline. Effie's son 'the Whistler' murders her husband. Part of Tales of My Landlord, purportedly edited by Jedediah Cleishbotham. Rob Roy: Frank Osbaldistone tracks down the disinherited Rashleigh in Scotland; the title Highlander disguises himself as the cattle dealer Campbell. The Bride of Lammermoor: Edgar of Ravenswood falls in love with a woman he saves from a bull; she stabs her husband on their wedding night and goes insane. Inspired Donizetti's Lucia di Lammermoor (with sextet 'Chi mi frena in tal momento'). Kenilworth: Amy Robsart is tricked into falling to her death by Richard Varney. Marmion: contains 'What a tangled web we weave, when first we practice to deceive!' The Lady of the Lake features Ellen Douglas singing Ave Maria; Roderick Dhu lights a burning cross, inspiring the KKK. Scott organized George IV's 1822 visit to Scotland. He cited the fictional antiquarian Dryasdust. Gyorgy Lukacs's Marxist reading praised Scott's mediocre heroes. E.M. Forster deemed him a mediocre novelist but good storyteller.

clues.clue: The Heart of Midlothian: Jeanie Deans walks to London to pardon sister Effie; Tales of My Landlord edited by Cleishbotham
examples: Jeanie walks barefoot to London to plead for the life of her sister Effie

clues.clue: Rob Roy: Frank Osbaldistone tracks Rashleigh; title outlaw disguises as Campbell
examples: Frank Osbaldistone goes to Scotland to track down Rashleigh

clues.clue: The Bride of Lammermoor: title character stabs husband on wedding night; inspired Lucia di Lammermoor
examples: stabs her husband on their wedding night

clues.clue: Marmion: 'What a tangled web we weave, when first we practice to deceive!'
examples: 'What a tangled web we weave, when first we practice to deceive!'

comprehensive_summary:
Sir Walter Scott was a Scottish novelist and poet often credited with inventing the historical novel. His works profoundly influenced 19th-century culture, particularly in the American South, where Mark Twain blamed Scott's romanticized chivalry for causing the Civil War.

Ivanhoe is his most frequently clued novel. The protagonist disguises himself as Desdichado at a tournament, defeats the Templar Brian de Bois-Guilbert, and wins Lady Rowena. He defends the Jewish Rebecca from being burned as a witch. The novel inspired Southern jousting culture, Charles Chesnutt's The House Behind the Cedars, and Thomas Dixon's cross-burning scenes in The Clansman.

Waverley is his first novel and often regarded as the first historical novel. Edward joins the Jacobite cause after visiting Donald Bean Lean and Flora MacIvor. Scott's later novels were published 'by the author of Waverley.' A steamboat in Huckleberry Finn is named after him.

The Heart of Midlothian follows Jeanie Deans walking barefoot to London to seek a pardon for her sister Effie. Rob Roy features Frank Osbaldistone tracking the disinherited Rashleigh. The Bride of Lammermoor inspired Donizetti's opera Lucia di Lammermoor. Marmion contains the famous line 'What a tangled web we weave.' The Lady of the Lake inspired the KKK's cross-burning through Roderick Dhu's scene. Scott organized George IV's 1822 tartan-heavy visit to Scotland and cited the fictional antiquarian Dryasdust.

cards.front: Novel: protagonist disguises as Desdichado; defeats Templar Brian de Bois-Guilbert; defends the Jewish Rebecca from being burned as a witch; marries Rowena
Author: Mark Twain blamed this novelist for the Civil War, saying he introduced 'the jejune romanticism of an absurd past' and created a 'namesake disease' in the South
Novel: often regarded as the first historical novel; protagonist joins the Jacobite cause after visiting Donald Bean Lean and Flora MacIvor
Novel: Jeanie Deans walks barefoot to London to seek a pardon for her sister Effie from Queen Caroline
cards.back: The Heart of Midlothian (Sir Walter Scott)

cards.front: Novel: Frank Osbaldistone tracks the disinherited Rashleigh; the title Highlander disguises himself as cattle dealer Campbell
Poem: contains 'What a tangled web we weave, when first we practice to deceive!'; published with antiquarian notes
